episode_title: Florent Daudens: Building an AI-Literate Newsroom at Radio Canada
transcript_file: 2024-03-29 - Florent Daudens Building an AI-Literate Newsroom at Radio Canada.txt
summary_claims: Only 10% of newsrooms train their journalists in AI, and 13% of newsrooms don't even consider training their staff in AI
In three months, approximately 100 people at Radio Canada received AI training

At the beginning of training sessions, journalists associate AI with words like fear, replacement, loss of control, and octopus; by the end, they use words like creativity, sense of control, and possibility
The training session scores among the highest for concrete applicability of learning to work

guests.name: Florent Daudens
guests.role: Director of News Gathering and Deployment

matches.theme_name: The Automation Paradox: What AI Replaces, What Remains Irreplaceable

best_quote.text: I think above all it's curiosity. Because a machine will not spontaneously ask itself a question. And the other thing is editorial judgment.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens articulates the human-AI boundary through the Prime Minister press conference example, showing how AI summarization misses editorial judgment, while demonstrating efficiency gains in transcription, content adaptation across platforms, and data extraction from scanned documents.
matches.matched_text: I think above all it's curiosity Because a machine will not spontaneously ask itself a question. And the other thing is editorial judgment. We discussed it before, but I think When there is editorial judgment involved, it will always remain something only and fundamental, fundamentally human.

matches.theme_name: Trust on the Line: Credibility, Transparency, and the Audience Contract

best_quote.text: Do we want to create an individual news offer for each people? Is it a bad idea? What will we lose in the trade-off between our goal is to inform the general public and have different ideas confronting in the public space. But also what if you as a user want something very specific?
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens raises the tension between personalized news and the public broadcasting mandate to inform broadly, touching on how audience consumption habits may shift with AI-powered devices and conversational interfaces.
matches.matched_text: Do we want to create an individual news offer for each people? Is it a bad idea? What will we lose in the in this kind of trade-off between, you know, our goal is to inform the general public and have different ideas confronting in the s in the public space. But also what if you as a user want something very specific?

matches.theme_name: The Ethics Infrastructure: From Principles on Paper to Practice in the Newsroom

best_quote.text: Sometimes you can't rely on summarization for an important news conference from the Prime Minister if you haven't heard it. If you haven't exercised your editorial judgment to know what was important for this press conference.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Ethics is woven into the training sessions rather than treated as a standalone topic, with Daudens emphasizing that AI should not be used when editorial judgment is at stake -- a practical ethical principle embedded in daily workflow decisions.
matches.matched_text: Sometimes you can't rely on summarization for an important news conference from the Prime Minister if you haven't heard it. If you haven't Exercise your editorial judgment to know what was important for this press conference. But for example, if you need the time code or if you need to just extract some quotes.

matches.theme_name: Building the Machine: Technical Architecture and Engineering Decisions

best_quote.text: An LLM is not a search engine and we shouldn't use it as a search engine. But then we see how to ground an LLM to a database or to the web search tool.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens demonstrates technical sophistication in distinguishing LLMs from search engines, building RAG-based teaching assistants with LangChain, evaluating French-language model performance on MMLU benchmarks, and noting that Mixtral outperforms GPT-4 in French.
matches.matched_text: an LLM is not a search engine and we don't we shouldn't use it as a search engine. But then we see how to ground an NLM to a database or to the web, the web search tool, for example, etc.

matches.theme_name: The Literacy Imperative: Upskilling the Newsroom for an AI Era

best_quote.text: If we don't know what we're talking about, if we don't know the possibilities, we will just miss the train and then realize that we have to use it but we don't know how and it's gonna be scary.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: This is the central theme: Daudens describes Radio Canada's three-tier literacy program (expertise group, three-hour training sessions, prompt clinics), showing how training shifts journalist attitudes from fear to creativity, while building a shared prompt library and arguing that individual ChatGPT use remains 'artisanal and not very effective' without collective sharing.
matches.matched_text: If we don't know what we're talking about, if we don't know the possibilities, we will just miss the train and then realize that we have to use it but we don't know how and it's gonna be scary, etc.

matches.theme_name: Organizing for AI: Leadership, Strategy, and Institutional Transformation

best_quote.text: We need to think ourselves as both content and tech companies. It's crucial, I think.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens advocates for a cultural identity shift where newsrooms see themselves as both content and tech companies, describing the cross-functional expertise group structure and the importance of collaboration between journalists and developers.
matches.matched_text: We need to think ourselves as both content and tech companies. It's it's crucial, I think.

matches.theme_name: Power, Money, and the Platform: Big Tech and the News Industry

best_quote.text: The French startup Mistral... their first model, Mistral 7B, it had only been trained in English and I must say I was surprised.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens briefly touches on cultural sovereignty issues with AI models, noting that even French AI startup Mistral initially trained only in English, and highlighting Montreal's unique position as an AI hub.
matches.matched_text: This is Newsroom Robots, the podcast where we explore the intersection of artificial intelligence and the news industry. I'm Nikita Roy, data scientist, media entrepreneur, and one of the many founders currently building their. Ventures at the Harvard Innovation Labs. On the Newsroom Robots, I'm excited to bring you insightful conversations with industry experts about how AI is impacting the way we do journalism. Joining me on the show today is Florent Daudens. He's the director of news gathering and deployment at Canada's national public broadcaster, Radio Canada. where he focuses on enhancing the news department with artificial intelligence. His work involves AI training and development in their newsroom, as well as managing operations across their national, parliamentary and foreign bureaus.

matches.theme_name: Reimagining the News Product: Personalization, Formats, and the Experience Layer

best_quote.text: Do we still focus on writing classical web articles or do we think of new ways of presenting information and really focus on how will people interact with devices, with platforms, and with information.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens envisions fundamental changes in how information is presented, distinguishing between content for audiences versus content for search engines/AI, and exploring personalized newspapers (GPT Newspaper), voice interfaces, and content adaptation across TV, radio, TikTok, and news apps.
matches.matched_text: do we still focus on writing classical web articles or do we think of ways of new ways of presenting information and and you know really focus on how will people interact will interact with devices, with platforms, and with information.

matches.theme_name: Survival Economics: Business Models, Revenue, and Market Position in the AI Era

best_quote.text: Our resources are increasingly limited. So if we manage to configure tools well, starting for example with a few shot prompts or if necessary with fine-tuning, we would be able to generate a web article draft from a TV text and it can be a significant gain for journalists.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Resource constraints at Radio Canada drive the use case for AI content adaptation, with Daudens noting that platforms to fill (TV, radio, TikTok, web, apps) keep growing while resources shrink.
matches.matched_text: our resources are increasingly limited. So if we manage to configure tools well, starting for example with a few shot prompts or if it's if necessary with fine-tuning, we would be able, for example, to generate a a web article draft from a TV the text and and it can be a significant gain for for journalists.

matches.theme_name: Regulating the Revolution: Law, Policy, and Journalism as Accountability

best_quote.text: There's also a lot of AI labels where it's not really AI. So we have to be able to differentiate true from false claims about AI.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens briefly notes the importance of journalists being able to identify genuine versus marketing AI claims, positioning informed journalism as a form of accountability.
matches.matched_text: there's also a lot of AI labels where it's not really AI. So we have to be able to differentiate true from from you know false. Claims about AI and it's yeah really important.

matches.theme_name: The Prototype Pipeline: From Experiment to Shipped Product

best_quote.text: We really want to do short concrete iterations and we will capitalize on that... we don't want to overload developers who don't have the time to work on it.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Daudens emphasizes short concrete iterations in the prompt clinic approach, creating working tools (prompts, Google Colabs, GPTs) rather than grand projects, and building prototypes that emerge from daily operations rather than top-down mandates.
matches.matched_text: we really want to do short concrete iterations and we will capitalize on that. And you know, for more structural projects, it's on the side of the digital media department.

matches.theme_name: Data Privacy, Source Protection, and the Boundaries of Responsible Use

best_quote.text: For the transcription of video or audio, models were primarily trained in English and they were therefore useless in French. And with the Quebec accent, the error rate was still high.
best_quote.speaker: Florent Daudens

matches.summary: Data quality concerns emerge through the lens of language representation -- models trained on French from France fail Quebec French speakers, raising questions about whose data and whose language gets represented in AI systems.
matches.matched_text: for the transcription of video or audio, models were primarily trained in English and they were therefore useless in French. Then they improved in French, but with the French accent. And with the Quebec accent, for example, the error rate was still high But then we saw big players like uh I think for example Amazon was the first to train its models with Quebec speakers and then it became really usable in production Today with models like Whisper from OpenAI, we see little difference.
